text: The surgeon exposes the pancreas by entering the lesser sac and cutting vasobrevia. Bipolar coagulation is used to control small vessels, while low-power monopolar coagulation and scissors are used to divide the omentum. Small clips are applied to control the vasobrevia.

The surgeon divides the phrenocolic ligament to mobilize the left colonic flexure. The tail of the pancreas is then smoothly dissected from the kidney along the avascular plane of the Gerota's fascia.

The surgeon dissects the main trunk of the splenic artery proximally to the lesion, passing a vessel loop under the artery. Traction on the loop is applied to facilitate dissection and ensure quick control in case of bleeding. Small vascular clamps are used during the delicate dissection of the artery.

The surgeon identifies a small arterial trunk directed to the lower pole of the spleen, which is immersed in the lesion. The trunk is ligated and divided after separating the main trunk of the artery from the pancreas.

The surgeon removes the vascular clamp as the dissection nears completion, ensuring safety. The small polar artery is divided distally to the lesion, revealing the lower proximal branch of the splenic vein.

The surgeon carefully divides the pancreatic tail, attempting to identify the pancreatic duct. The duct is very thin at this level and often not visible, but in this case, it is identified and directly ligated.

The surgeon progressively dissects the tail of the pancreas away from the splenic vein using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, along with bipolar coagulation. A venous branch is identified, ligated, and divided, completing the resection without any intraoperative bleeding.

A hemostatic and sealing patch is applied to the pancreatic stump. The surgical specimen is placed in a retrieval bag and extracted through a small incision. A small drain is positioned in the left hypochondrium for postoperative drainage.
